steps.title: Use the evidence gap
steps.content: The passage says many historians of capitalism rely on longitudinal economic data from archival records. It also says those records do not exist for much of precolonial Africa. Duarte and other Africanist scholars still show that capitalist features existed there before colonization, so the supported inference is that they likely draw on evidence outside the archival data usually favored by those historians.

steps.title: Reject unsupported differences
steps.content: Choice A - They likely make use of different types of evidence than historians of capitalism typically rely on follows from the missing archival record base. Choice B - They likely differ from historians of capitalism in the methods they use to derive longitudinal economic data from archival records still depends on records the passage says largely do not exist. Choice C - They likely have a different view about which activities should be considered capitalist in nature invents a definitional dispute. Choice D - They likely view capitalism as having been more beneficial for Africa than historians of capitalism do shifts to benefits, which the passage does not discuss. The answer is A.
